ground_truth.title: HR Shared Services explained: Benefits, functions, and best practices
ground_truth.author: Elliot Raba
ground_truth.main_content:
HR shared services have evolved into a cornerstone of modern HR operating models, enabling organisations to drive both efficiency and value creation at scale. Far from being just a cost-control tactic, this unique model helps organisations simplify delivery, enhance compliance and ensure consistent employee experiences across geographies.

As employees and organisations increasingly expect scalable, people-centred services that support strategic growth, HR shared services are transforming how organisations balance operational efficiency with strategic HR delivery. The 2024 ScottMadden Benchmarking Report reveals that leading organisations service up to 456 full-time employees with just one shared service staff member. This underscores the model's scalability and effectiveness. Notably, two-thirds of organisations using HR shared services are prioritising multifunction shared service models. This highlights a clear shift toward integrated, agile, and future-ready HR operations.

HR shared services refer to a centralised unit that manages routine HR processes and organisational transactions. These often include payroll, benefits administration, employee data management, onboarding, and helpdesk support. Rather than duplicating these services in every region or business unit, shared services consolidate them into one location or digital platform. According to Deloitte, over 80% of large organisations have adopted some form of shared services, highlighting the maturity and broad acceptance of the model.

The main components that enable this model to work efficiently include a multi-tiered support model (self-service, helpdesk, specialist support), workflow automation, case and knowledge management tools, data integration and reporting platforms, and robust change management to support adoption. In practice, an HR shared services model operates as a central service hub, which can be a physical, virtual, or a hybrid model. Services are typically structured in tiers: Tier 0 for self-service portals, Tier 1 for contact centres, Tier 2 for HR specialists, and Tier 3 for strategic HR or Centre of Excellence teams.

Key benefits include cost efficiency and scalability through consolidation and automation, which has reduced administration fees by 21% according to ISG's benchmarking report. Shared services also provide consistency and compliance through standardised processes across regions, improved employee experience through accessible self-service and responsive support, better data visibility and insights for informed decision-making, and the ability to enable strategic HR by freeing up professionals from routine tasks.

Implementation challenges include change resistance from employees and managers, technology limitations with legacy systems, data quality issues, undefined SLAs, and cultural differences in multinational organisations. Solutions include clear communication, technology upgrades, data cleansing and governance, well-defined SLAs, and tailored training.

Best practices for implementation include securing leadership sponsorship, mapping current processes to identify inefficiencies, designing a clear service catalogue and SLA framework, investing in change management, starting with a pilot region, and continuously measuring performance. Technology should support workflow automation, knowledge and case management, multi-channel employee access, integration across systems, and configurable SLAs.

The case study of Innomotics demonstrates how HR shared services can support rapid transformation. When Innomotics became an independent company with 15,000+ employees across 40+ countries, partnering with Zalaris to set up a centralised HR shared services model enabled standardisation of core HR processes while maintaining local compliance, reducing administrative effort and improving employee experience.

HR shared services are more than a cost-saving initiative, they are a strategic lever for improving HR efficiency, agility, and experience at scale.
